title: Edwards Lifesciences EV1000 Clinical Platform Hemodynamic Monitor - No Pressure Waveform or Inaccurate Arterial Pressure Display
description: Troubleshooting absent, dampened, unstable, or inaccurate arterial pressure waveform display from external setup, cable, sensor, or patient connection issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot the EV1000 setup on an active patient if the displayed arterial pressure is being used for clinical decision-making and appears unreliable. Action: Notify clinical staff that the arterial pressure reading may be inaccurate. Have staff confirm patient status using another approved method if needed. Do not disconnect invasive pressure lines unless clinical staff are managing the line. If monitoring is clinically required, move the patient to another working monitor setup or alternate pressure monitoring method. Expected outcome: Patient care continues without relying on an unverified invasive pressure reading. Why it matters: An inaccurate arterial pressure waveform can lead to incorrect treatment decisions.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the issue is no waveform, incorrect numeric pressure, dampened waveform, noisy waveform, or intermittent signal. Action: Observe the EV1000 display. Confirm whether the arterial pressure parameter is missing, flatlined, noisy, dampened, or numerically inaccurate. Ask clinical staff when the problem started: During setup. After patient movement. After cable change. After sensor replacement. During transport or repositioning. Check for visible alarms, prompts, or sensor messages. Expected outcome: The failure mode is clearly identified before parts are swapped. Why it matters: A missing waveform and an inaccurate waveform can have different causes.

steps.title: Confirm the Patient Line and Pressure Source
steps.instructions: Verify that the arterial line and pressure setup are clinically valid before focusing on the EV1000 hardware. Action: Have clinical staff confirm the arterial line is patent. Confirm the pressure tubing is connected to the correct patient line. Check for obvious kinks, clamps, air bubbles, loose connections, or blood backup. Confirm the flush bag has pressure and fluid. Confirm the stopcock position is correct for monitoring. Expected outcome: The patient pressure source is present and able to transmit pressure. Why it matters: A monitor cannot display a reliable waveform if the pressure line is blocked, clamped, disconnected, or improperly positioned. If this resolves the issue, document the line/setup problem and stop.

steps.title: Check the Edwards Sensor or Transducer Setup
steps.instructions: Inspect the connected FloTrac, Acumen IQ, or compatible pressure sensor setup. Action: Confirm the sensor is the correct type for the EV1000 setup being used. Check that the sensor is fully connected to the pressure cable. Inspect the sensor connector for fluid, bent pins, cracked housing, or contamination. Confirm the sensor is not expired, damaged, or previously used inappropriately. Replace the sensor if clinical staff agree and the current sensor is suspect. Expected outcome: The EV1000 recognizes the pressure sensor and displays a usable arterial pressure waveform. Why it matters: Sensor recognition and signal quality depend on a clean, secure, compatible sensor connection. If this resolves the issue, document the sensor issue and stop.

steps.title: Check the Pressure Cable Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the cable between the EV1000 and the pressure sensor. Action: Confirm the pressure cable is connected securely at both ends. Disconnect and reconnect the cable to ensure it is fully seated. Inspect the cable for cuts, crushed sections, bent pins, loose strain reliefs, or fluid contamination. Gently flex the cable near the connectors while observing for signal dropout. Swap with a known-good compatible pressure cable if available. Expected outcome: A known-good cable restores a stable waveform and accurate pressure display. Why it matters: Intermittent pressure waveforms are often caused by worn cables or poor connector seating. If this resolves the issue, document the cable failure and stop.

steps.title: Confirm Correct Pressure Channel or Parameter Selection
steps.instructions: Verify that the pressure source is assigned and displayed correctly on the EV1000. Action: Check that the arterial pressure parameter is selected or active on the display. Confirm the correct pressure input is being viewed. Make sure the waveform is not hidden, minimized, or assigned to a different display area. Confirm the patient monitoring mode and connected sensor type match the intended setup. Expected outcome: The arterial pressure waveform appears in the expected display location. Why it matters: A connected sensor may appear missing if the wrong parameter, screen layout, or channel is selected. If this resolves the issue, document the configuration issue and stop.

steps.title: Verify Leveling and Zeroing
steps.instructions: If the waveform is present but the numeric pressure appears inaccurate, verify the pressure system was leveled and zeroed correctly. Action: Have clinical staff level the transducer to the appropriate reference point. Open the stopcock to air as clinically appropriate. Perform a zeroing procedure according to facility practice. Return the stopcock to the monitoring position. Confirm the waveform and numeric values after zeroing. Expected outcome: The pressure values become clinically reasonable and waveform baseline is corrected. Why it matters: Incorrect leveling or zeroing can cause pressure values to read falsely high or low even when the monitor is functioning properly. If this resolves the issue, document the leveling or zeroing correction and stop.

steps.title: Assess Waveform Quality
steps.instructions: If the waveform is present but dampened, over-dynamic, noisy, or inconsistent, focus on the fluid path and setup before suspecting monitor failure. Action: Ask clinical staff to check for air bubbles in the tubing. Check for kinked, loose, or excessively long pressure tubing. Confirm the flush device functions correctly. Confirm the pressure bag is inflated properly. Check whether patient movement, line position, or catheter condition is affecting the waveform. Compare against another monitor or pressure module if available. Expected outcome: The waveform becomes sharp, consistent, and clinically usable after correcting the external setup. Why it matters: Poor waveform quality is commonly caused by line, tubing, catheter, or transducer setup issues rather than the EV1000 itself. If this resolves the issue, document the setup correction and stop.

steps.title: Check the EV1000 Hardware and Module Connections
steps.instructions: If external setup appears correct, inspect the EV1000 platform and connected modules. Action: Confirm the EV1000 is powered normally. Check for system messages related to pressure cable, sensor, module, or communication failure. Reseat external modules or connection points if applicable. Inspect the EV1000 ports for damage, bent pins, looseness, or fluid intrusion. Test the same cable and sensor setup on another compatible monitor if available. Test the EV1000 with a known-good compatible setup if available. Expected outcome: The fault follows either the external setup or remains with the EV1000 platform. Why it matters: Swapping known-good components helps separate a cable/sensor issue from a monitor or port failure.

steps.title: Perform a Safe Functional Check
steps.instructions: After any correction, verify the monitor is stable before returning it to use. Action: Confirm the EV1000 recognizes the sensor. Confirm the arterial pressure waveform is present. Confirm the numeric pressure updates appropriately. Confirm no active sensor, cable, or pressure-related alarms remain. Confirm the display remains stable after gentle cable movement. Have clinical staff confirm the pressure value is clinically reasonable. Expected outcome: The EV1000 displays a stable arterial waveform and usable pressure values. Why it matters: A one-time signal return is not enough if the cable, sensor, or connector is intermittent. If this resolves the issue, return the device to service according to facility policy.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported that the EV1000 Clinical Platform was not displaying an arterial pressure waveform during patient setup.
CCR.Cause: Found the pressure cable not fully seated at the Edwards pressure sensor connection; waveform returned after reseating and securing the connection.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated pressure cable, inspected connector for damage, verified stable arterial waveform and numeric pressure display, and returned unit to service.

helpfulDetails: Patient removed from affected monitoring setup before troubleshooting
Exact symptom reported: no waveform, dampened waveform, noisy waveform, or inaccurate value
Sensor type used, such as FloTrac or Acumen IQ if applicable
Stopcock position verified by clinical staff
Ports checked for damage or fluid intrusion

title: Edwards Lifesciences EV1000 Clinical Platform Hemodynamic Monitor - FloTrac / Acumen IQ sensor not detected or setup failure
description: Troubleshooting FloTrac or Acumen IQ sensor detection, setup, cable, connection, and patient-data entry issues on the EV1000 platform.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Before troubleshooting, confirm the monitor is not being relied on as the only source of hemodynamic data. Notify clinical staff that the EV1000 is not detecting the FloTrac / Acumen IQ sensor correctly. Do not troubleshoot cable or sensor connections during critical patient instability unless clinical staff confirms it is safe. If advanced hemodynamic monitoring is needed immediately, have staff use another available monitor or alternate monitoring method. Confirm arterial pressure monitoring remains available through an appropriate bedside monitor if clinically required. Expected outcome: Patient care continues safely while the monitoring issue is evaluated. Why it matters: FloTrac / Acumen IQ data may support clinical decisions involving cardiac output, stroke volume, SVV, and pressure-based parameters.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Sensor Detection Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact symptom before replacing parts. Check whether the EV1000 displays a message such as sensor not detected, cable error, setup incomplete, unavailable parameter, or invalid configuration. Ask staff when the issue started: During initial setup, After sensor change, After cable movement, After patient transfer, After power cycle. Confirm whether the issue affects only FloTrac / Acumen IQ parameters or the entire EV1000 display. Expected outcome: The failure is narrowed to sensor detection, setup, cable connection, or system-level communication. Why it matters: A missing parameter may be caused by setup status, patient data entry, sensor connection, pressure signal quality, or cable failure.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Sensor and Monitoring Setup
steps.instructions: Verify that the sensor and setup match the intended monitoring mode. Confirm the sensor being used is compatible with the EV1000 platform configuration. Verify the clinical setup uses the correct arterial line connection. Confirm the sensor is properly connected between the arterial pressure line and the EV1000 interface cable. Check that the sensor is not expired, damaged, contaminated, or visibly compromised. Replace the disposable sensor if packaging, labeling, or physical condition is questionable. Expected outcome: A correct and usable sensor is installed for the intended monitoring function. Why it matters: The monitor cannot calculate advanced parameters if the disposable sensor is incorrect, damaged, or not properly connected.

steps.title: Inspect the Sensor Cable Connection
steps.instructions: Check the easiest physical failure point first. Disconnect and reconnect the FloTrac / Acumen IQ sensor cable. Confirm the connector is fully seated. Inspect the connector for bent pins, damaged locking tabs, fluid intrusion, cracked housing, or loose strain relief. Make sure the cable is not sharply bent, stretched, pinched, or routed where it may be pulled. Gently move the cable while watching for intermittent detection. Expected outcome: The sensor is detected and remains detected without intermittent dropouts. If the sensor is detected and the setup proceeds normally, document the loose or poor connection and stop. Why it matters: Sensor detection failures are commonly caused by incomplete seating, cable stress, or connector damage.

steps.title: Check the EV1000 Interface Cable and Module Connection
steps.instructions: Confirm the connection path from the disposable sensor back to the monitor. Trace the cable from the sensor back to the EV1000. Confirm the cable is connected to the correct EV1000 port or interface module. Reseat the cable at the monitor or module end. Inspect the port for damage, debris, fluid exposure, or looseness. Avoid forcing connectors into ports. Expected outcome: The EV1000 recognizes the connected sensor after all external connections are reseated. Why it matters: The disposable sensor may be good, but the monitor will not detect it if the reusable interface cable or port connection is poor.

steps.title: Swap with Known-Good External Accessories
steps.instructions: Use substitution to separate sensor, cable, and monitor issues. Replace the disposable FloTrac / Acumen IQ sensor with a known-good compatible sensor. If available, swap the reusable sensor cable with a known-good cable. Test using the same EV1000 monitor. If the issue follows the cable or sensor, remove that accessory from use. If the issue remains with the EV1000, continue troubleshooting the monitor setup. Expected outcome: The faulty external accessory is identified or ruled out. Why it matters: Replacing the monitor before ruling out the disposable sensor and reusable cable can waste time and create unnecessary downtime.

steps.title: Verify Required Patient and Setup Data
steps.instructions: Confirm that the setup is complete enough for parameters to display. Review the EV1000 setup screen. Confirm required patient information has been entered as needed, such as height, weight, age, sex, or other setup fields required by the monitoring mode. Confirm the correct monitoring technology or parameter set is selected. Verify there are no incomplete setup prompts or unresolved configuration messages. Have clinical staff confirm patient-specific entries when needed. Expected outcome: The monitor accepts the setup and begins displaying available hemodynamic parameters. Why it matters: Some parameters may remain unavailable if required setup fields are missing or the wrong monitoring mode is selected.

steps.title: Confirm Arterial Pressure Signal Quality
steps.instructions: Sensor detection and parameter calculation depend on a usable pressure signal. Confirm an arterial pressure waveform is present. Check for a flat, dampened, noisy, or over-ranged waveform. Verify the transducer/sensor has been leveled and zeroed by clinical staff according to facility practice. Confirm stopcocks, pressure tubing, flush device, and line setup are correct. Ask clinical staff to address line-related issues if the waveform appears poor. Expected outcome: A stable arterial pressure waveform is available and advanced parameters can calculate. Why it matters: The sensor may be detected, but FloTrac / Acumen IQ values may fail or appear unreliable if the arterial waveform is poor.

steps.title: Power Cycle the EV1000 Safely
steps.instructions: Clear a possible temporary software or communication state. Confirm with clinical staff that it is safe to interrupt the EV1000 display. Power down the EV1000 normally. Wait briefly, then power the system back on. Reconnect the sensor and cable if needed. Recheck sensor detection and setup status. Expected outcome: The EV1000 reloads normally and detects the connected sensor. If the issue clears and does not return, document the reset and verify normal operation before returning the monitor to use. Why it matters: A temporary software state or communication lockup may prevent proper sensor recognition.

steps.title: Check for Repeated Failure Across Multiple Sensors and Cables
steps.instructions: Determine whether the monitor itself is suspect. Test the EV1000 with a known-good sensor and known-good cable. If possible, test the same sensor and cable combination on another compatible monitor or module. Compare behavior: If the accessory works on another system, suspect the EV1000 port, module, or internal communication. If the problem follows the accessory, remove the accessory from service. Record any error messages, alarm behavior, and setup screens. Expected outcome: The failure is isolated to the disposable sensor, reusable cable, module/port, or EV1000 platform. Why it matters: Proper isolation prevents unnecessary monitor repair and helps repair teams reproduce the issue.

steps.title: Inspect for Fluid Damage or Physical Damage
steps.instructions: Look for signs that the device should not remain in service. Inspect the EV1000, module, sensor cable, and connectors for: Fluid residue, Cracked plastic, Bent pins, Loose ports, Damaged cable jacket, Burn marks, Unusual heat or smell. Do not continue using any component with suspected fluid intrusion or electrical damage. Expected outcome: Damaged parts are removed from service and the monitor is escalated if needed. Why it matters: Fluid or connector damage can cause intermittent detection, inaccurate monitoring, or electrical safety concerns.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported the EV1000 would not detect the FloTrac / Acumen IQ sensor during arterial line setup.
CCR.Cause: Found the reusable sensor cable intermittently losing connection when flexed near the connector; known-good sensor and cable worked normally.
CCR.Resolution: Removed defective cable from service, verified EV1000 detected a known-good FloTrac / Acumen IQ setup, and returned monitor to service.

helpfulDetails: Exact sensor detection message or setup error
Whether issue occurred at startup, during setup, or during patient monitoring
Zeroing/leveling status confirmed by clinical staff
Any fluid damage, bent pins, loose connector, heat, smell, or physical damage

title: Edwards Lifesciences EV1000 Clinical Platform Hemodynamic Monitor - Cardiac output, stroke volume, SVV, or hemodynamic parameters missing or unreliable
description: Troubleshooting missing or unreliable cardiac output, stroke volume, SVV, or related hemodynamic values from setup, sensor, cable, waveform, or patient-data issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot the EV1000 on an actively monitored patient unless clinical staff confirm it is safe. Verify the patient is being monitored by another reliable source if hemodynamic values are unavailable. Notify clinical staff that advanced parameters may not be dependable. Do not use questionable cardiac output, SV, SVV, or derived values for clinical decisions until resolved. If the patient is unstable, prioritize alternate monitoring and clinical escalation. Expected outcome: Patient care continues without relying on missing or unreliable EV1000 values. Why it matters: Advanced hemodynamic parameters depend on accurate signal input, patient setup, and sensor data. Bad data can be more dangerous than no data.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the problem is truly missing data, intermittent data, inaccurate-looking values, or values that do not match the patient condition. Ask staff which parameter is affected: cardiac output, cardiac index, stroke volume, stroke volume variation, SVR, or other calculated values. Confirm whether the issue started at setup, after cable or sensor change, after patient repositioning, during transport, or after monitor reboot. Observe whether values are blank, dashed, frozen, unstable, or obviously incorrect. Expected outcome: The failure pattern is clearly identified before replacing parts or escalating. Why it matters: Missing values often point to setup or connection issues, while unreliable values may point to waveform quality, patient data, or physiologic limitations.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Monitoring Configuration
steps.instructions: Verify that the EV1000 is configured for the intended hemodynamic monitoring mode. Confirm the correct patient monitoring screen or application is selected. Verify the required technology or sensor type is selected, such as FloTrac-based arterial pressure monitoring if applicable. Confirm the connected sensor/module matches the expected monitoring method. Check for setup prompts, incomplete configuration steps, or unresolved onscreen messages. Expected outcome: The EV1000 recognizes the intended monitoring setup and displays the correct parameter fields. Why it matters: Advanced parameters may not populate if the monitor is not configured for the correct measurement type.

steps.title: Check the Arterial Pressure Waveform Quality
steps.instructions: For FloTrac/arterial-based parameters, verify the arterial pressure waveform is present, accurate, and properly scaled. Confirm the arterial waveform is visible and pulsatile. Look for dampened, over-damped, under-damped, noisy, or artifact-heavy waveforms. Ask clinical staff to verify the arterial line is patent and clinically appropriate. Confirm the transducer is leveled and zeroed according to clinical policy. Check that the pressure value shown on the EV1000 is reasonable compared with the bedside monitor if available. Expected outcome: A clean arterial waveform is present and the displayed pressure is clinically reasonable. Why it matters: Cardiac output, stroke volume, and SVV calculations depend heavily on accurate arterial waveform input. A poor waveform can cause missing or unreliable derived values. If correcting the waveform resolves the missing or unreliable parameters, return the device to service and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect the Sensor and Patient Cable Connections
steps.instructions: Check all external connections before suspecting a monitor failure. Inspect the FloTrac/Acumen-style sensor connection if used. Verify the sensor is fully seated into the EV1000 cable. Inspect the cable connector for bent pins, fluid contamination, cracked housing, or loose fit. Disconnect and reconnect the sensor and cable firmly. Confirm the cable is connected to the correct EV1000 port or module. Expected outcome: The EV1000 detects the sensor/cable and begins displaying available parameters. Why it matters: A partially seated sensor or damaged cable can allow some pressure information while preventing advanced parameters from calculating correctly. If reseating the cable restores the values, document the connection issue and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Sensor or Cable
steps.instructions: Rule out disposable sensor or cable failure using known-good components. Replace the disposable sensor if clinically appropriate and approved by staff. Swap the patient cable with a known-good EV1000-compatible cable. Avoid reusing a questionable sensor or cable on another patient setup. Watch for parameter return after the swap. Expected outcome: The monitor recognizes the replacement component and values return if the issue was accessory-related. Why it matters: Sensors and interface cables are common external failure points and should be ruled out before removing the platform from service. If the known-good cable or sensor resolves the issue, remove the defective accessory from use and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify Required Patient Data and Setup Entries
steps.instructions: Confirm the monitor has the required patient information for calculated values. Check patient demographic entries such as height, weight, age, sex, and body surface area if used by the configuration. Confirm the patient category and setup selections are correct. Review any prompts asking for missing patient information. Correct obvious setup errors only according to site policy and clinical workflow. Expected outcome: Calculated values populate once required patient data is complete and valid. Why it matters: Some hemodynamic parameters require patient-specific data. Missing or incorrect entries can prevent values from appearing or make calculated values unreliable.

steps.title: Check for Physiologic or Clinical Limitations
steps.instructions: Determine whether the values are unreliable because the measurement conditions are not appropriate. Ask clinical staff whether the patient has rhythm irregularity, severe artifact, mechanical support, excessive movement, or unstable vascular conditions. Confirm whether SVV is expected to be valid for the patient’s current ventilation and rhythm status. Review whether the displayed values change appropriately with the arterial waveform and patient condition. Do not attempt to “fix” physiologic limitations as an equipment issue. Expected outcome: Clinical staff confirm whether the parameter is appropriate for the current patient condition. Why it matters: Some advanced parameters are only meaningful under certain physiologic conditions. The monitor may function normally while the value is not clinically reliable.

steps.title: Check for Alarms, Messages, or Sensor Recognition Errors
steps.instructions: Review the EV1000 for messages related to pressure, sensor, cable, module, or parameter availability. Look for onscreen prompts or error messages. Note the exact wording of any cable, sensor, pressure, or parameter message. Confirm whether only one parameter is missing or multiple values are affected. Record whether the error clears after reseating or swapping accessories. Expected outcome: Any active message is identified and tied to a specific external cause if possible. Why it matters: Error text helps separate a patient setup problem from a sensor, cable, module, or internal platform issue.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Safe
steps.instructions: If the setup appears correct and the patient is not relying on the monitor, perform a controlled restart. Confirm with clinical staff that restarting the EV1000 will not affect active patient care. Power the monitor down using the normal shutdown process. Wait briefly, then power it back on. Allow the system to fully boot and reconnect to the sensor/cable setup. Confirm whether parameters return. Expected outcome: Temporary software or communication issues clear after restart. Why it matters: A controlled reboot can restore communication between the platform and connected accessories without unnecessary repair escalation. If the restart restores normal values and the issue does not recur, document the reset and verify operation before returning the device to service.

steps.title: Compare Against Another Monitor or Known-Good Setup
steps.instructions: If values remain questionable, compare the setup with another known-good monitoring source. Compare arterial pressure display against the bedside monitor if connected to the same pressure source. Use a known-good EV1000 cable or compatible setup if available. Confirm whether the issue follows the cable/sensor or remains with the EV1000 platform. Do not rely on advanced values until the source of the discrepancy is understood. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to the patient setup, accessory chain, or EV1000 platform. Why it matters: Comparison testing helps avoid condemning the monitor when the actual problem is the patient line, sensor, cable, or waveform source.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Edwards EV1000 was not displaying cardiac output, stroke volume, or SVV during arterial pressure monitoring.
CCR.Cause: Found arterial waveform present but dampened and the FloTrac sensor connection was loose at the patient cable.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated sensor connection, verified clean arterial waveform with staff, confirmed cardiac output, stroke volume, and SVV values returned, and left unit in service.

helpfulDetails: Whether values were blank, dashed, frozen, or unstable
Alarm behavior or EV1000 onscreen messages
Whether cable or sensor was swapped
Power behavior and whether the monitor was restarted
Environmental factors such as transport, movement, or setup changes
Indicator lights or connection status messages
Whether transducer was leveled and zeroed by clinical staff

title: Edwards Lifesciences EV1000 Clinical Platform Hemodynamic Monitor - Module, cable, or patient connection error
description: Troubleshooting EV1000 module, cable, sensor, or patient connection errors caused by setup, accessory, or connection problems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot the EV1000 on an active patient if monitoring data is needed for immediate clinical decisions. Notify clinical staff that the EV1000 connection issue is being evaluated. Confirm whether the patient is being actively monitored with another reliable source. If the EV1000 data is critical, have staff move monitoring to another available monitor or platform before troubleshooting. Do not disconnect invasive pressure lines, sensors, or patient cables without clinical awareness. Expected outcome: Patient monitoring remains safe and uninterrupted while the connection issue is investigated. Why it matters: Module or cable errors may interrupt pressure, cardiac output, or advanced hemodynamic data used during patient care.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact error before replacing parts or removing the device from service. Ask staff what message appeared on the EV1000 display. Note whether the issue involves module not detected, cable not detected, sensor not connected, patient connection error, intermittent connection, or parameter unavailable. Confirm whether the issue started after patient setup, transport, cable change, module change, or cleaning. Expected outcome: The reported issue is narrowed to the affected module, cable, sensor, or patient connection. Why it matters: A general connection complaint may be caused by the EV1000 monitor, the databox/module, the sensor, the patient cable, or the clinical setup.

steps.title: Check Power and Basic System Status
steps.instructions: Confirm the EV1000 platform is fully powered and not in a startup or communication fault state. Verify the EV1000 monitor is powered on. Confirm the display is responsive. Check for active system alarms, startup errors, or communication messages. If safe and not actively relied upon, power-cycle the monitor and allow it to fully restart. Expected outcome: The system boots normally and recognizes connected accessories. If this resolves the issue, document the restart and return the device to service only after normal operation is confirmed.

steps.title: Inspect the External Module and Cable Connections
steps.instructions: Check all external connections before assuming a failed module or cable. Verify the module or databox is fully seated and connected. Disconnect and reconnect the external cable firmly. Check that connectors are aligned correctly and not partially inserted. Inspect the connector ends for bent pins, damaged contacts, fluid residue, corrosion, or cracked housings. Confirm no cable is under tension, pinched, stretched, or sharply bent. Expected outcome: The EV1000 recognizes the connected module or cable without intermittent dropouts. Why it matters: Loose or contaminated connectors are common causes of module and patient connection errors.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Edwards Cable or Sensor Is Being Used
steps.instructions: Verify that the accessory matches the intended monitoring function. Confirm the connected cable or sensor is appropriate for the EV1000 setup being used. Verify the clinical team is not using a damaged, expired, incompatible, or incorrect sensor/cable. Check for obvious labeling mismatch between the selected monitoring mode and the attached accessory. If available, compare against a known-good EV1000-compatible cable or sensor. Expected outcome: The monitor is connected to the correct accessory for the selected parameter. Why it matters: The EV1000 may not detect or accept the wrong cable, sensor, or module configuration.

steps.title: Inspect the Patient Cable or Sensor Connection
steps.instructions: Check the patient-side connection without disturbing active patient care. Confirm the sensor or pressure cable is connected fully to the patient cable or module. Check for loose luer connections, disconnected sensor cables, or hanging cable strain. Inspect the disposable sensor connection point for moisture, damage, or incomplete seating. If staff recently changed the transducer, FloTrac sensor, or pressure setup, verify the new setup was connected correctly. Expected outcome: The patient-side connection is secure and the monitor detects the expected signal. If this resolves the issue, stop and document the connection correction.

steps.title: Check for Cable Damage
steps.instructions: Look for physical damage that could cause intermittent communication or detection failure. Inspect the full length of the cable. Look for cuts, crushed sections, exposed shielding, kinks, pulled strain reliefs, or taped repairs. Gently flex the cable near the connector while observing whether the error appears or clears. Do not aggressively manipulate the cable if it is connected to a patient. Expected outcome: Cable integrity is confirmed, or a damaged cable is identified. If damage is found, remove the cable from service and replace it with a known-good cable.

steps.title: Swap With a Known-Good Cable or Module
steps.instructions: Use substitution testing to isolate the failed external part. Replace the suspected cable with a known-good compatible cable. If the issue remains, test the suspected module or databox on another known-good EV1000 setup if available. If a known-good module works on the same monitor, suspect the original module or cable. If the same error follows the cable or module, remove that accessory from service. Expected outcome: The issue either follows the accessory or remains with the EV1000 system. Why it matters: Controlled swapping helps avoid unnecessary monitor replacement.

steps.title: Confirm the Monitor Configuration and Parameter Selection
steps.instructions: Verify the EV1000 is set up for the parameter the clinical team expects to monitor. Confirm the selected monitoring mode matches the attached cable, module, and sensor. Verify the expected pressure or hemodynamic parameter is enabled and visible. Check whether the screen layout is hiding the expected value or waveform. Confirm the issue is truly a connection problem and not a display-layout or setup-selection issue. Expected outcome: The EV1000 is configured to display the connected patient parameter. If this resolves the issue, document the setup correction and educate staff as appropriate.

steps.title: Check for Fluid Intrusion or Cleaning Damage
steps.instructions: Inspect for signs of damage from cleaning or fluid exposure. Look for moisture around connectors, module ports, cable ends, and sensor interfaces. Check for sticky residue, dried fluid, corrosion, or damaged connector shells. Ask whether the issue began after cleaning, turnover, or fluid exposure. Do not reconnect visibly wet or contaminated accessories. Expected outcome: Fluid-related connector issues are ruled out or identified. If fluid intrusion is suspected, remove the affected part or device from service for bench evaluation.

steps.title: Test the EV1000 in a Controlled Non-Patient Setup
steps.instructions: If available, use a known-good external setup to verify basic detection. Move the EV1000 to a safe bench or non-patient setting if it is not needed for active care. Connect known-good power, module, cable, and compatible test setup. Observe whether the EV1000 detects the module or cable consistently. Check for repeatable errors during startup and after gentle cable movement. Expected outcome: The EV1000 either detects the known-good setup normally or continues to report connection errors. Why it matters: A known-good test setup helps separate clinical setup problems from equipment faults.

steps.title: Return to Service Only After Stable Operation Is Verified
steps.instructions: Do not return the device based only on a cleared error message. Confirm the EV1000 detects the module, cable, or sensor repeatedly. Verify the expected parameter appears without intermittent dropout. Check that no connection error returns after cable movement or restart. Clean the device as appropriate before returning it to the clinical area. Expected outcome: The EV1000 operates normally with stable accessory detection.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported the EV1000 displayed a cable connection error and would not recognize the patient monitoring cable.
CCR.Cause: Found the pressure cable connector partially seated at the EV1000 interface with no visible damage to the cable or port.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated the cable, verified stable module detection and patient parameter display, checked for intermittent dropout, and returned the EV1000 to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether the issue involved module, cable, sensor, or patient connection
Whether the problem occurred at startup or during patient monitoring
Alarm behavior or active system messages
Power behavior during startup or restart
Environmental factors, cleaning, turnover, or fluid exposure
Indicator lights, display messages, or module detection status
Any bent pins, fluid residue, corrosion, or cable damage found
Whether the error followed the cable, module, or monitor
Whether the device was returned to service or removed for repair

title: Edwards Lifesciences EV1000 Clinical Platform Hemodynamic Monitor - Touchscreen, display, boot, or communication failure
description: Troubleshooting EV1000 touchscreen, display, startup, power, boot, or communication problems caused by external setup, power, cabling, or system connection issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Before troubleshooting, confirm whether the EV1000 is actively being used for patient monitoring. Action: Do not troubleshoot on an actively monitored patient unless it is clinically safe. If hemodynamic monitoring is required, have clinical staff move monitoring to another approved system. If the EV1000 display is blank, frozen, or unreliable, do not rely on it for clinical decisions. Remove the unit from active use if the issue affects monitoring accuracy, visibility, or alarm awareness. Expected outcome: Patient monitoring continues safely without relying on an unstable or nonfunctional EV1000 system. Why it matters: A frozen display, boot failure, or communication issue can prevent clinicians from seeing accurate hemodynamic information.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Failure
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly what problem is occurring before replacing parts or escalating. Action: Ask staff what they observed: Touchscreen not responding. Display blank. Unit will not power on. Unit powers on but will not boot fully. System freezes. Communication failure with connected monitor, module, or data output. Note whether the issue occurred: At startup. During patient monitoring. After being moved. After cable changes. After cleaning. After a power interruption. Expected outcome: The failure mode is clearly identified. Why it matters: Power, display, boot, touchscreen, and communication failures can look similar but have different external causes.

steps.title: Check AC Power Source
steps.instructions: Start with the wall power source and power path. Action: Confirm the EV1000 power cord is fully seated at the device and outlet. Verify the outlet is live using an outlet tester or known-good device. Check whether the outlet is controlled by a switch, power strip, or emergency power circuit. Move the unit to a known-good hospital-grade outlet if needed. Inspect the power cord for damage, loose fit, bent prongs, or signs of overheating. Expected outcome: The EV1000 powers on normally from a verified power source. If this resolves the issue, return the device to service after confirming normal startup and operation.

steps.title: Check Power Supply and Power Indicators
steps.instructions: Determine whether the device is receiving power. Action: Look for any power indicator lights on the system or power supply. Listen for startup sounds, fan activity, relay clicks, or screen backlight behavior. Confirm the power button is functioning normally and not stuck. If an external power supply is used, inspect it for damage, unusual heat, or loose cable connections. Swap with a known-good compatible power cord or power supply only if available and approved by your facility. Expected outcome: Power input or power accessory issues are either corrected or ruled out. Why it matters: A failed cord, loose power connection, or faulty external supply can mimic a major system failure.

steps.title: Perform a Safe Power Cycle
steps.instructions: A locked-up system may recover after a proper restart. Action: Remove the device from patient use first. Power the EV1000 down normally if possible. Disconnect AC power. Wait long enough for the system to fully shut down. Reconnect power and attempt a normal startup. Observe whether the unit boots fully, freezes, shows an error, or remains blank. Expected outcome: The EV1000 restarts and reaches the normal operating screen. If this resolves the issue, monitor the system briefly for stability before returning it to use.

steps.title: Inspect the Display
steps.instructions: If the unit powers on but the screen is blank or unreadable, check for external display-related issues. Action: Confirm the display brightness is not turned down. Look closely for a faint image or backlight failure. Inspect the screen for cracks, pressure damage, liquid intrusion, or cleaning residue. Check whether the screen changes during startup. Confirm whether the system is frozen or only the display is affected. Expected outcome: Display visibility issues are identified as either setting-related, contamination-related, or likely hardware-related. Why it matters: A device may be running internally even if the display is not usable.

steps.title: Check Touchscreen Response
steps.instructions: If the screen displays normally but does not respond, rule out simple causes first. Action: Remove gloves only if safe and appropriate for bench testing. Clean and dry the screen using approved cleaning methods. Check for cleaning residue, moisture, adhesive, cracked glass, or screen protector problems. Confirm the issue affects the entire touchscreen or only one area. Restart the system after cleaning and drying the touchscreen. Expected outcome: The touchscreen responds normally after cleaning, drying, and restart. If the touchscreen remains unresponsive, remove the unit from service.

steps.title: Check Connected Cables, Modules, and Accessories
steps.instructions: A connected accessory or communication cable may prevent normal operation or trigger communication errors. Action: Disconnect nonessential external cables. Inspect all patient cables, pressure cables, monitor interface cables, communication cables, and module connections. Look for bent pins, damaged connectors, broken locking tabs, corrosion, fluid contamination, or loose fit. Reconnect one cable or accessory at a time. Use known-good compatible accessories when available. Expected outcome: A bad accessory, damaged cable, or loose connection is identified. If a specific cable or module causes the failure, replace or remove that accessory and retest the EV1000.

steps.title: Check Network or Data Communication Connections
steps.instructions: For communication failure, verify the external data path before assuming the EV1000 has failed. Action: Confirm the Ethernet or communication cable is connected to the correct port. Inspect the cable for damage or loose latch tabs. Try a known-good network cable. Confirm the wall jack or network port is active if applicable. Check whether other nearby devices are communicating normally. Verify the issue is not isolated to a downstream system, interface engine, patient monitor, or EMR connection. Expected outcome: External network, cable, and port issues are ruled out or corrected. Why it matters: Communication failures often come from disconnected cables, inactive ports, network changes, or downstream interface problems.

steps.title: Check for Error Messages or Startup Faults
steps.instructions: If the system powers on but does not complete startup, capture the exact fault behavior. Action: Record any displayed error messages, fault codes, or startup screens. Note whether the system loops, freezes, shuts down, or stops at a specific screen. Check whether the failure occurs with all accessories disconnected. Photograph the error if allowed by facility policy. Do not clear repeated errors without documenting them. Expected outcome: The failure is documented clearly for repair evaluation or vendor support. Why it matters: Exact startup behavior helps separate software lockup, accessory communication issues, display problems, and internal hardware faults.

steps.title: Confirm Date, Time, and Configuration Only If the System Boots
steps.instructions: If the EV1000 boots but has communication or data transfer issues, check basic configuration. Action: Verify date and time are correct. Confirm network configuration has not been changed. Confirm the correct connection type is being used for the intended data path. Check whether the device was moved from another location with different network settings. Compare settings with a known-good EV1000 if available and allowed by facility procedure. Expected outcome: Configuration-related communication problems are corrected or ruled out. Why it matters: A device may function locally but fail communication if network or interface settings are incorrect.

steps.title: Retest Normal Operation
steps.instructions: After correcting any external issue, verify stable operation. Action: Restart the EV1000. Confirm the display is readable. Confirm touchscreen response. Confirm the system completes boot. Confirm expected cables or modules are detected. Confirm communication status if network/export is part of the complaint. Allow the unit to remain powered on long enough to confirm the problem does not immediately return. Expected outcome: The EV1000 operates normally and is safe to return to service. If the issue is resolved, document the findings and return the device to service according to facility procedure.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Edwards EV1000 touchscreen was frozen and the system would not respond during setup.
CCR.Cause: Verified the unit powered on, but the touchscreen remained unresponsive after cleaning, power cycling, and disconnecting external cables.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the EV1000 from service, labeled it Out of Service, documented the touchscreen failure, and sent the unit for bench evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Alarm behavior or displayed startup fault
Accessories swapped, including power cord, cables, modules, or communication cables
Power behavior, including outlet tested, power cord inspected, power indicator status, startup sounds, or shutdown behavior
Environmental factors such as cleaning residue, moisture, fluid contamination, movement, network changes, or power interruption
Indicator lights, display condition, touchscreen response, startup screen, or error message observed
Final device status, including whether the unit was returned to service, removed from service, labeled Out of Service, or sent for bench evaluation

title: Edwards Lifesciences HemoSphere Advanced Monitoring System Hemodynamic Monitor - Pressure Cable, Sensor, or Module Not Detected
description: Troubleshooting when the HemoSphere system does not recognize a pressure cable, sensor, module, or connected hemodynamic accessory.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Before troubleshooting, confirm whether the monitor is being used for active invasive hemodynamic monitoring. Action: Do not troubleshoot cable or module connections on an active patient unless clinical staff confirm it is safe. If pressure or hemodynamic data is clinically needed, have staff move monitoring to another available device or backup method. If the monitor is not displaying required parameters during patient care, treat the issue as clinically significant. Expected outcome: Patient monitoring is maintained safely while the equipment issue is investigated. Why it matters: A missing pressure cable, sensor, or module can prevent arterial pressure, central venous pressure, cardiac output, or other advanced parameters from displaying.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Detection Issue
steps.instructions: Confirm what the HemoSphere system is failing to detect. Action: Ask clinical staff what was connected when the issue occurred. Identify whether the issue involves a pressure cable, pressure transducer or sensor, FloTrac / Acumen IQ sensor, HemoSphere module, patient cable, smart cable, or interface cable. Check whether the message appears at startup, after connection, during patient monitoring, or after moving the monitor. Expected outcome: The exact failed item and timing are identified. Why it matters: A missing module message, cable detection error, and sensor recognition issue can have different causes.

steps.title: Check the Module Seating
steps.instructions: Confirm the module is fully installed in the monitor. Action: Remove the monitor from patient use first if needed. Power the unit down if required by facility practice before reseating modules. Inspect the module bay. Remove and reseat the module firmly. Confirm the module locks or seats evenly without being partially inserted. Restart the monitor and check whether the module is recognized. Expected outcome: The HemoSphere system detects the installed module. If resolved, stop troubleshooting and document the reseated module. Why it matters: A partially seated module may appear physically installed but fail electrical communication with the monitor.

steps.title: Inspect External Cable Connections
steps.instructions: Check all external connections before suspecting equipment failure. Action: Confirm the pressure cable is connected to the correct port or module. Disconnect and reconnect the cable. Check for loose, angled, or partially inserted connectors. Inspect the connector ends for bent pins, damaged contacts, fluid contamination, cracked plastic, or broken locking tabs. Confirm no adapter or extension cable is missing from the setup. Expected outcome: The cable connects securely and the monitor recognizes the accessory. If resolved, stop troubleshooting and document the loose or corrected connection. Why it matters: Detection issues are often caused by connection problems, damaged connector contacts, or incorrect cable routing.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Sensor or Disposable Is Being Used
steps.instructions: Verify that the connected sensor matches the intended HemoSphere setup. Action: Confirm the disposable pressure sensor, FloTrac sensor, Acumen IQ sensor, or related accessory is compatible with the monitor configuration. Check that the sensor packaging is correct for the intended parameter. Confirm the sensor is not expired, damaged, contaminated, or previously used in a way that could affect recognition. Replace the disposable sensor with a new compatible sensor if there is any doubt. Expected outcome: The monitor detects the correct sensor after connection. If resolved, stop troubleshooting and document the defective, incorrect, or replaced sensor. Why it matters: Some detection problems come from the wrong disposable, damaged sensor contacts, or a sensor that cannot communicate properly with the cable/module.

steps.title: Swap With a Known-Good Cable
steps.instructions: Rule out the pressure cable or smart cable as the cause. Action: Use a known-good compatible cable from another working HemoSphere setup if available. Connect the known-good cable to the same module and sensor. Check whether the monitor now detects the cable or sensor. If the known-good cable works, tag the suspect cable for replacement or repair. Expected outcome: The monitor recognizes the known-good cable. If resolved, stop troubleshooting and remove the failed cable from service. Why it matters: Cables are high-wear items and may fail from repeated cleaning, bending, pulling, or connector damage.

steps.title: Swap With a Known-Good Sensor or Module When Available
steps.instructions: Narrow the failure to the sensor, cable, module, or monitor. Action: Test the suspect cable with a known-good compatible sensor. Test the suspect sensor with a known-good compatible cable if appropriate. If the system uses a removable module, test with another known-good compatible module if one is available. Do not interchange parts unless they are confirmed compatible and safe for that monitor configuration. Expected outcome: The failed component is isolated. If resolved, stop troubleshooting and remove the failed accessory or module from service. Why it matters: Controlled swapping helps avoid replacing the monitor when the issue is actually a failed cable, module, or disposable sensor.

steps.title: Check for Contamination or Cleaning Damage
steps.instructions: Look for signs that fluid or cleaning solution affected the connector or module area. Action: Inspect cable ends, module ports, and the monitor connection area. Look for moisture, residue, corrosion, sticky buildup, or damaged seals. Do not connect wet or contaminated accessories. Allow only approved cleaning and drying methods per facility policy. Remove any accessory from service if contamination or corrosion is found. Expected outcome: Connectors are clean, dry, undamaged, and safe to reconnect. Why it matters: Fluid intrusion or residue can prevent accessory detection and may create unreliable monitoring.

steps.title: Restart the Monitor
steps.instructions: Clear a possible temporary software or communication hang. Action: Disconnect from patient use first if the monitor is being used clinically. Power the HemoSphere system off. Wait briefly. Power the monitor back on. Reconnect the module, cable, and sensor. Confirm whether the device detects the accessory after restart. Expected outcome: The accessory is detected after the system reinitializes. If resolved, stop troubleshooting and document that the issue cleared after restart and reconnection. Why it matters: Some accessory communication errors may persist until the monitor restarts and rechecks connected hardware.

steps.title: Check Monitor Configuration and Parameter Setup
steps.instructions: Confirm the monitor is set up for the intended monitoring mode. Action: Verify the expected parameter is enabled or selected. Confirm the correct module or cable type is being used for that parameter. Check whether the patient profile, monitoring mode, or connection screen shows the accessory as unavailable, disconnected, or unsupported. Have clinical staff confirm the intended monitoring setup if needed. Expected outcome: The monitor configuration matches the connected hardware and intended parameter. Why it matters: The monitor may not display or recognize expected data if the wrong mode, setup path, or parameter configuration is selected.

steps.title: Check Power and General System Health
steps.instructions: Make sure the detection problem is not part of a larger system issue. Action: Confirm the monitor powers on normally. Check for boot errors, system alerts, battery issues, touchscreen problems, or repeated restarts. Confirm the module bay and display respond normally. Listen for unusual sounds and check for unusual heat or smell. Expected outcome: The monitor operates normally except for the specific detection issue. Why it matters: Multiple system problems may indicate a larger internal fault rather than a single cable or sensor issue.

steps.title: Perform a Known-Good Setup Test
steps.instructions: Use a complete known-good setup to confirm whether the monitor itself is the problem. Action: On the bench, connect a known-good compatible module, cable, and sensor. Power on the monitor. Confirm whether the HemoSphere system detects the full setup. If the known-good setup fails, suspect the monitor, module bay, or internal communication path. Expected outcome: The monitor detects the known-good setup. If the known-good setup works, the original accessory chain is the likely issue. If it fails, escalate the monitor for repair. Why it matters: A complete known-good setup is one of the cleanest ways to separate accessory failure from monitor failure.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Edwards HemoSphere monitor would not detect the connected pressure cable and sensor.
CCR.Cause: Inspection found the pressure cable was not recognized on the original setup, but the monitor detected a known-good cable and sensor during bench testing.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the failed pressure cable from service, verified the HemoSphere monitor recognized a known-good cable/sensor setup, and returned the monitor to service.

helpfulDetails: Exact message or detection error displayed
Whether issue involved pressure cable, sensor, module, or smart cable
Parameter affected, such as arterial pressure, CVP, cardiac output, SVV, or advanced monitoring value
Whether issue occurred at startup, during setup, or during patient monitoring
Alarm behavior or alerts present during the issue
Accessories swapped, including known-good cable, sensor, or module
Power behavior, restart results, boot errors, battery issues, or repeated restarts
Environmental factors such as fluid exposure, cleaning residue, moisture, or connector contamination
Indicator lights, system messages, connection screen status, or module status indicators

title: Edwards Lifesciences HemoSphere Advanced Monitoring System Hemodynamic Monitor - Invasive Pressure Waveform Absent, Dampened, or Inaccurate
description: Troubleshooting missing, dampened, inaccurate, or unreliable invasive pressure waveforms caused by setup, cable, sensor, leveling, zeroing, or connection issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot the pressure monitoring setup on an unstable patient unless clinical staff confirms it is safe. Confirm whether the pressure value is being used for active patient management. Have clinical staff verify the patient’s condition using another approved method if the pressure reading is questionable. If the waveform is absent or inaccurate during active use, have staff move monitoring to another working setup if needed. Do not disconnect invasive lines unless clinical staff is present and responsible for the line. Expected outcome: Patient care continues safely while the equipment issue is isolated. Why it matters: An inaccurate arterial, CVP, or PA pressure waveform can affect clinical decisions.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Symptom
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the issue is a total loss of waveform, dampened waveform, incorrect numeric value, or intermittent dropout. Observe the HemoSphere display. Confirm which pressure is affected: ART, CVP, PAP, or another invasive pressure channel. Ask when the issue began: during setup, after patient movement, after line change, after cable movement, after zeroing, or after transport. Check whether the waveform is flat, noisy, dampened, drifting, or present with incorrect numbers. Expected outcome: The failure mode is clearly identified before parts are swapped.

steps.title: Check the Pressure Cable Connection
steps.instructions: The HemoSphere system uses pressure cable and sensor/transducer connections for invasive pressure monitoring, so loose or incorrect cable connections can cause missing or unreliable values. Edwards setup material references connecting the HemoSphere pressure cable, sensor/transducer, leveling, and zeroing as part of pressure monitoring setup. Confirm the HemoSphere pressure cable is fully seated at the monitor. Inspect the cable connector for bent pins, fluid contamination, cracked housing, or loose fit. Confirm the sensor/transducer connector is fully seated into the pressure cable. If available, try another known-good HemoSphere pressure cable. Expected outcome: The pressure channel detects the cable and sensor properly. If the waveform returns after reseating or replacing the cable, document the connection or cable issue and stop.

steps.title: Check the Disposable Pressure Sensor or Transducer
steps.instructions: A failed, expired, damaged, wet, or incorrectly connected disposable sensor can cause no waveform or inaccurate readings. Confirm the correct Edwards-compatible pressure sensor/transducer is being used. Inspect the sensor connector for fluid, damage, or poor fit. Confirm the sensor is not expired or visibly damaged. Verify the sensor is connected to the correct pressure cable and patient monitoring setup. If the sensor is suspected, have clinical staff replace it using hospital policy. Expected outcome: A known-good sensor provides a stable pressure signal.

steps.title: Verify Tubing, Stopcocks, and Line Setup
steps.instructions: Dampened or inaccurate invasive pressure waveforms are commonly caused by line setup issues rather than monitor failure. Confirm all tubing connections are tight. Check for air bubbles in the tubing or stopcocks. Confirm the stopcock position is correct for monitoring. Look for kinked, compressed, clotted, or excessively long tubing. Confirm the line is not partially disconnected or leaking. Have clinical staff flush or correct the line setup per hospital policy. Expected outcome: The waveform becomes sharper, more responsive, and clinically believable. If the waveform improves after correcting tubing, stopcock, or bubble issues, document the external cause and stop.

steps.title: Verify Pressure Bag and Flush System
steps.instructions: For fluid-filled invasive pressure monitoring, an underinflated pressure bag or restricted flush setup can cause dampened waveforms. Confirm the flush bag is pressurized to the expected clinical setup level, commonly 300 mmHg when used with compatible arterial pressure monitoring setups. Check that the drip chamber and flush system are not empty, blocked, or leaking. Confirm clinical staff can perform an appropriate flush response if needed. Do not manipulate invasive pressure lines without clinical staff involvement. Expected outcome: The system maintains proper dynamic response and waveform quality.

steps.title: Check Sensor Leveling
steps.instructions: Incorrect leveling can cause inaccurate pressure values even when the waveform shape appears normal. Confirm the sensor/transducer is leveled to the correct anatomical reference point, typically the phlebostatic axis for invasive hemodynamic pressure monitoring. Check whether the bed height, patient position, or transducer holder was moved. Re-level the sensor with clinical staff if needed. Expected outcome: The numeric pressure value becomes more accurate after proper leveling. Why it matters: A waveform can look present but still display the wrong pressure if the transducer is not level.

steps.title: Check Zeroing
steps.instructions: An unzeroed or incorrectly zeroed pressure channel can create inaccurate values or misleading baseline pressure. Confirm the pressure channel was zeroed after setup. Verify the stopcock was opened to air during zeroing, if applicable. Confirm the monitor shows the channel as zeroed with current date/time if available. Re-zero the pressure channel with clinical staff using hospital policy. Expected outcome: The waveform baseline and numeric pressure are corrected. If the issue resolves after proper zeroing, document the zeroing issue and stop.

steps.title: Check Pressure Type and Channel Assignment
steps.instructions: Wrong pressure type selection or channel assignment can cause confusion, incorrect labeling, or incorrect interpretation. Confirm the pressure source is assigned to the correct parameter type, such as ART, CVP, or PAP. Verify the displayed pressure label matches the actual line connected. Confirm the waveform color, label, and patient monitor routing match the intended pressure source. Correct the pressure type or channel assignment if it was misconfigured. Expected outcome: The displayed waveform and numeric value match the connected invasive pressure source.

steps.title: Compare Against Another Monitor or Known-Good Setup
steps.instructions: Use substitution to separate monitor/cable failure from patient line or disposable setup issues. If available, connect the pressure setup to another compatible monitor or known-good HemoSphere setup. Or connect a known-good pressure cable and sensor setup to the suspect HemoSphere monitor. Compare whether the problem follows the cable/sensor/setup or stays with the monitor. Expected outcome: The likely failed component is identified without internal disassembly.

steps.title: Check for Display, Freeze, or Software Behavior
steps.instructions: If the waveform appears frozen, delayed, or intermittently missing, the issue may not be the pressure line itself. Confirm the monitor is not in monitoring pause or a setup screen that prevents normal waveform display. Check whether other parameters are updating normally. Restart the monitor only if it is safe and not actively needed for patient care. Confirm whether the issue returns after restart. Expected outcome: Temporary display or software-related behavior is ruled out.

steps.title: Inspect for Physical Damage or Fluid Intrusion
steps.instructions: Hemodynamic monitoring equipment is used around fluids, pressure bags, and invasive line setups. Inspect the monitor, pressure cable, and connectors for fluid exposure. Check for cracked connectors, damaged cable strain relief, bent pins, or sticky residue. Remove damaged or contaminated accessories from service. Do not continue using a cable or monitor with suspected fluid intrusion. Expected outcome: Unsafe or unreliable accessories are removed before further patient use.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported the HemoSphere monitor had no arterial pressure waveform and displayed unreliable invasive pressure values during setup.
CCR.Cause: Found the pressure transducer cable connection loose at the HemoSphere pressure cable, causing intermittent waveform detection.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated the pressure cable connection, verified stable invasive pressure waveform with clinical staff, confirmed proper leveling and zeroing, and returned the monitor to service.

helpfulDetails: Pressure type affected: ART, CVP, PAP, or other
Whether waveform was absent, dampened, noisy, drifting, or flat
Whether numeric pressure was missing or inaccurate
Tubing, stopcocks, and air bubbles checked
Any error messages or detection issues
Whether issue followed cable/sensor or stayed with monitor

title: Edwards Lifesciences HemoSphere Advanced Monitoring System Hemodynamic Monitor - Cardiac Output Or Advanced Parameter Values Missing / Unreliable
description: Troubleshooting missing, unstable, or questionable cardiac output and advanced hemodynamic values caused by setup, sensor, cable, module, or signal issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot the HemoSphere system in a way that delays patient care or compromises active monitoring. Confirm whether the patient is actively depending on the displayed cardiac output, stroke volume, SVV, ScvO₂, or other advanced parameter values. If the values are missing, frozen, or clinically questionable, notify the clinical team and have them use alternate assessment methods or another monitor if needed. Do not disconnect invasive lines, catheters, or patient sensors without clinical approval. Expected outcome: Patient care continues safely while Clinical Engineering evaluates the equipment side of the issue. Why it matters: Advanced hemodynamic values may influence treatment decisions. Unreliable data should not be treated as accurate until verified.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly which value is missing or unreliable. Ask staff which parameter is affected, such as: cardiac output, cardiac index, stroke volume, SVV, SVR, ScvO₂, Acumen / advanced decision support values, or pressure-derived parameters. Check whether the issue is: no value displayed, dashed value, intermittent value, value present but clinically unrealistic, parameter grayed out, sensor or module not detected, or value not updating. Expected outcome: The failure is narrowed to a specific parameter, module, cable, sensor, or setup path. Why it matters: “Cardiac output not working” can be caused by several different external issues depending on which monitoring technology is being used.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Monitoring Setup Is Selected
steps.instructions: Verify that the HemoSphere monitor is set up for the intended monitoring mode and connected technology. Check that the correct module, cable, or monitoring mode is selected for the clinical setup being used. Confirm that the expected parameter is supported by the connected sensor, cable, and module. Verify that the patient profile and monitoring configuration are active and not in a setup or paused state. Expected outcome: The monitor is in the correct mode and capable of displaying the requested parameter. Why it matters: Some advanced values only appear when the correct Edwards module, pressure cable, sensor, or oximetry setup is connected and configured.

steps.title: Check the Patient Cable, Module, and Connector Seating
steps.instructions: Inspect all external connections before assuming a monitor failure. Check the front and rear module connections. Disconnect and reconnect the appropriate Edwards cable or module if safe and approved by clinical staff. Inspect connectors for: bent pins, loose fit, cracked housing, fluid contamination, cable strain, and damaged locking tabs. Expected outcome: The module and cable are fully seated and recognized by the monitor. Why it matters: A loose or damaged cable can cause missing values, intermittent readings, or unreliable hemodynamic calculations.

steps.title: Check for Sensor or Disposable Setup Issues
steps.instructions: Confirm that the correct disposable sensor or patient connection is being used and properly connected. Verify that the FloTrac, Acumen IQ, pressure transducer, oximetry cable, or other connected sensor is appropriate for the intended parameter. Confirm the sensor is connected securely to the cable. Check for expired, damaged, wet, or previously used disposables if applicable. Have clinical staff confirm the invasive line or catheter setup is clinically appropriate and functional. Expected outcome: The disposable sensor path is intact and suitable for measurement. Why it matters: Many missing or unreliable values are caused by sensor setup, line condition, or disposable issues rather than the monitor itself.

steps.title: Verify Pressure Waveform Quality If Pressure-Derived Values Are Affected
steps.instructions: If cardiac output or advanced parameters are calculated from arterial pressure, confirm the waveform is usable. Observe the arterial pressure waveform on the monitor. Look for: flat waveform, dampened waveform, overly sharp waveform, excessive artifact, intermittent dropout, incorrect scale, and questionable zero or leveling issue. Have clinical staff verify the pressure line, transducer position, flush system, stopcock position, and zeroing. Expected outcome: The pressure waveform is clean, stable, and clinically appropriate. Why it matters: Pressure-derived cardiac output and advanced parameters depend on waveform quality. A poor waveform can produce missing or unreliable values. If waveform correction resolves the issue, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Confirm Required Patient Data Is Entered Correctly
steps.instructions: Some calculated values require accurate patient demographics or setup data. Check that required patient information is entered correctly, such as: height, weight, age, sex, BSA-related fields, and monitoring mode selections. Correct any obvious setup omissions with clinical approval. Expected outcome: The monitor has the required information to calculate indexed or advanced values. Why it matters: Missing or incorrect patient data can prevent certain calculated values from appearing or can make displayed values unreliable.

steps.title: Check Alarm, Message, and Status Notifications
steps.instructions: Review any on-screen messages that explain why a value is unavailable. Look for displayed messages such as sensor not detected, poor signal quality, invalid waveform, setup incomplete, cable fault, module fault, or parameter unavailable. Record the exact wording of any message before clearing or power cycling the monitor. Expected outcome: The displayed message identifies the likely affected accessory, module, sensor, or setup condition. Why it matters: The monitor may already be identifying the failed portion of the measurement chain.

steps.title: Swap External Accessories With Known-Good Parts
steps.instructions: Use known-good external parts when available. If clinically safe and approved, test with: known-good Edwards cable, known-good module, new compatible disposable sensor, known-good pressure cable, and known-good oximetry cable if applicable. Do not reuse patient disposables inappropriately. Expected outcome: The issue follows a cable, module, or sensor, or remains with the monitor. Why it matters: Controlled accessory swaps help separate monitor failure from external connection or disposable failure. If a known-bad accessory is identified, remove it from service and stop.

steps.title: Restart the Monitor Only When Clinically Safe
steps.instructions: If the display is not updating, values appear frozen, or the system behavior is abnormal, perform a controlled restart only when patient monitoring can safely continue another way. Ask clinical staff whether monitoring can be interrupted. Move the patient to alternate monitoring if needed. Power cycle the HemoSphere monitor. Allow the system to fully boot. Reconnect the module and cables. Confirm whether the missing or unreliable values return. Expected outcome: Temporary software or communication issues clear after restart. Why it matters: A restart can correct a temporary system lockup, but it should never interrupt critical patient monitoring without a safe backup.

steps.title: Check Power and Battery Status
steps.instructions: Confirm the monitor has stable power. Verify the power cord is fully connected. Check the outlet or power strip if applicable. Confirm the monitor is not unexpectedly running on battery. Look for low battery, charging, or power-related warnings. Expected outcome: The monitor has stable AC power and is not operating under a power-related fault condition. Why it matters: Unstable power can contribute to system errors, unexpected shutdowns, or unreliable module communication.

steps.title: Check Network or Data Export Only If the Values Are Present Locally
steps.instructions: If the values appear correctly on the HemoSphere monitor but are missing in the EMR, central display, or data export system, separate the issue from bedside measurement. Confirm whether the value is present on the HemoSphere screen. If present locally but missing elsewhere, check: network connection, data export cable, docking or interface connection, bed assignment, interface status, and EMR mapping or connectivity queue. Expected outcome: The problem is identified as a connectivity/export issue rather than a measurement issue. Why it matters: A value can be clinically valid on the monitor but still fail to export because of network or interface problems.

steps.title: Perform a Basic Functional Check Off Patient
steps.instructions: If the issue cannot be resolved at the bedside, remove the monitor from patient use and test it in a safe non-patient environment. Use known-good external accessories if available. Verify the monitor boots normally. Check whether modules are detected. Check whether expected screens, parameter selections, and status messages behave normally. Do not perform invasive patient-line troubleshooting on the bench. Expected outcome: Clinical Engineering determines whether the issue is accessory-related, configuration-related, or likely internal to the monitor. Why it matters: Bench evaluation prevents unnecessary patient disruption and supports better repair decisions.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Edwards HemoSphere monitor was not displaying cardiac output and advanced hemodynamic values during patient monitoring.
CCR.Cause: Found arterial waveform quality was poor due to an external pressure setup issue, preventing reliable pressure-derived parameter calculation.
CCR.Resolution: Clinical staff corrected the pressure setup, waveform returned to normal, cardiac output and advanced parameter values repopulated, and monitor remained in service.

helpfulDetails: Whether the value was absent, dashed, frozen, or intermittent
Whether the outlet or AC power was checked
Whether cables or modules were swapped

title: Edwards Lifesciences HemoSphere Advanced Monitoring System Hemodynamic Monitor - Network / Export / EMR Connectivity Failure
description: Troubleshooting HemoSphere network, data export, EMR, interface, or connectivity failures caused by setup, cabling, network, or configuration issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the HemoSphere monitor is not the only source being used for active patient decision-making before troubleshooting connectivity. Action: Do not interrupt monitoring on an active patient unless clinical staff confirm it is safe. If the monitor is actively being used for hemodynamic monitoring, leave patient monitoring intact. Troubleshoot network/export issues only if it does not affect active bedside monitoring. If needed, have staff manually document values until connectivity is restored. Expected outcome: Patient monitoring continues safely even if EMR export or network communication is unavailable. Why it matters: A connectivity issue is usually secondary to the primary monitoring function. Patient safety and continued visibility of hemodynamic values come first.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Connectivity Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly what is failing before changing settings. Action: Ask clinical staff what is not working: No values crossing into the EMR. Delayed values. Wrong patient association. No network connection. Export failure. HL7/interface issue. Data not reaching a central system. Confirm whether the monitor is still displaying patient parameters locally. Check whether the issue affects one HemoSphere monitor or multiple devices. Expected outcome: The failure is narrowed to a single monitor, patient association issue, network issue, or larger interface/EMR issue. Why it matters: One device failing points toward local cabling/configuration. Multiple devices failing points toward network, interface engine, EMR, or server-side problems.

steps.title: Check the Network Cable and Physical Connection
