steps.instructions: Start with the simplest external connection. Action: Inspect the Ethernet/network cable at the HemoSphere monitor. Confirm the cable is fully seated. Check for damaged connectors, broken tabs, bent cable strain relief, or loose wall jack connections. If safe and available, swap with a known-good network cable. Check for link lights at the network port, if visible. Expected outcome: The monitor has a secure physical network connection. Why it matters: A loose or damaged Ethernet cable can cause complete loss of export, intermittent communication, or delayed data transmission. If this resolves the issue, return the device to service and document the cable or connection problem.

steps.title: Verify the Network Jack or Drop
steps.instructions: Confirm the wall jack or network drop is active. Action: Plug the network cable into the correct clinical network jack if multiple ports are present. Avoid using unlabeled, inactive, guest, or non-clinical network ports. Test the port with an approved network tester or coordinate with IT if Clinical Engineering does not manage network validation. Compare against a nearby working HemoSphere monitor if available. Expected outcome: The monitor is connected to an active, correct network port. Why it matters: Network/export failures are often caused by the device being connected to the wrong jack, an inactive port, or a port assigned to the wrong network/VLAN. If the jack is inactive or incorrectly configured, escalate to IT/network support.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Patient and Bed Association
steps.instructions: Verify the monitor is associated with the correct patient/location workflow. Action: Check whether the HemoSphere monitor is assigned to the correct patient, room, bed, or location according to facility workflow. Confirm clinical staff selected or admitted the correct patient if the system uses patient association. Check whether the EMR is expecting data from a specific bed, device ID, or location. Compare the monitor location with the EMR/interface expectation. Expected outcome: The device, patient, and bed/location match the expected EMR destination. Why it matters: The monitor may be working correctly but exporting data to the wrong patient context, wrong location, or not exporting because patient association is incomplete. If this resolves the issue, confirm values are crossing correctly before closing the work order.

steps.title: Check Network Status or Connectivity Indicators
steps.instructions: Review available system status information without making deep configuration changes. Action: Check the HemoSphere system status screen or connectivity/network status indicators, if available. Look for messages related to network unavailable, export failed, connection lost, server unavailable, or communication error. Record any exact error messages. Note whether the device has an IP address, if visible through normal user-accessible screens. Expected outcome: The monitor provides a clear indication of whether it is connected, disconnected, or unable to communicate with a destination system. Why it matters: Exact messages help separate physical network failure from interface, server, configuration, or EMR-side issues.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only if Clinically Safe
steps.instructions: Restart the monitor only when it will not interrupt active patient monitoring. Action: Confirm with clinical staff that it is safe to restart the monitor. If connected to a patient, transfer monitoring or wait until the patient is no longer actively dependent on the display. Power the monitor down normally. Wait briefly, then power it back on. Allow the system to fully boot and reconnect. Check whether export or EMR communication resumes. Expected outcome: Temporary software or communication lockups clear after restart. Why it matters: Some communication failures are caused by temporary software, network handshake, or session problems. A safe restart can restore function without repair. If this resolves the issue, monitor briefly to confirm values continue exporting.

steps.title: Compare With Another Known-Good Monitor
steps.instructions: Determine whether the issue follows the device or the network location. Action: Move the suspected HemoSphere monitor to a known-good network location if safe and appropriate. Or connect a known-good HemoSphere monitor to the same network jack. Do not swap devices on active patients unless coordinated with clinical staff. Expected outcome: If the same monitor fails in a known-good location, suspect device configuration or internal communication issue. If a known-good monitor also fails at the same jack, suspect network drop, VLAN, IT configuration, or interface routing. Why it matters: This is one of the best ways to separate a device problem from a network infrastructure problem.

steps.title: Check for Recent Changes
steps.instructions: Look for timing clues. Action: Ask when the issue started. Check whether there were recent changes to: Network ports. EMR/interface system. Room/bed mapping. Device replacement. Software updates. IP/network configuration. Patient admission/discharge workflow. Ask whether other networked medical devices are affected. Expected outcome: A recent change may explain why communication stopped. Why it matters: Connectivity failures often happen after network changes, interface engine work, room moves, or patient association changes rather than hardware failure.

steps.title: Escalate to IT / Interface Support When Appropriate
steps.instructions: Involve the correct support team once local device checks are complete. Action: Contact IT, clinical informatics, or the interface/EMR support team if: The network jack is inactive. Multiple monitors are affected. The device has network link but no EMR data export. Patient/bed mapping appears incorrect. HL7/interface messages are not reaching the EMR. The issue started after a network or EMR change. Provide the device asset number, serial number, location, IP address if available, error message, and time of failure. Expected outcome: Network, interface, or EMR-side causes are investigated by the responsible team. Why it matters: Clinical Engineering can verify the device and external setup, but EMR routing, network switch configuration, firewall rules, and interface engine problems usually require IT or informatics support.

steps.title: Verify Restoration Before Closing
steps.instructions: Confirm that the repair or correction actually restored data flow. Action: Confirm the monitor displays patient values locally. Confirm the expected values export to the EMR or destination system. Verify the correct patient and bed/location. Ask clinical staff to confirm they can see the expected data. Document whether the issue was resolved locally, escalated, or left pending IT/interface action. Expected outcome: Data is flowing to the correct destination and clinical staff confirm normal operation. Why it matters: Network communication may appear restored at the device but still fail at the EMR or patient association level.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Edwards HemoSphere monitor in OR 4 was displaying values locally but not exporting hemodynamic data to the EMR.
CCR.Cause: Found Ethernet connection secure and monitor operating normally, but network drop did not provide active clinical network connectivity.
CCR.Resolution: Verified monitor operation on a known-good network jack, escalated inactive wall port to IT, and returned the monitor to service once EMR data export was confirmed.

helpfulDetails: Patient monitoring status at time of call
Exact reported export or EMR issue
Alarm behavior or connectivity warnings observed
Whether values displayed normally on the HemoSphere screen
IP address or network status, if available
Whether one monitor or multiple monitors were affected
Power behavior before and after restart, if performed
Environmental factors such as room move, network port change, or recent EMR/interface change

title: Edwards Lifesciences HemoSphere Advanced Monitoring System Hemodynamic Monitor - Touchscreen, battery, boot, or system software issue
description: Troubleshooting HemoSphere touchscreen, battery, startup, boot, display, charging, or software lockup issues before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the monitor is not being relied on for active hemodynamic decision-making before troubleshooting. If the HemoSphere monitor is connected to a patient, notify clinical staff before touching cables or power. If monitoring is unreliable, have staff move the patient to another approved monitoring method. Do not reboot or power-cycle the system during active patient monitoring unless clinical staff confirm it is safe. If values are frozen, missing, or questionable, treat the monitor as unreliable until verified. Expected outcome: Patient monitoring is maintained safely without depending on a questionable system. Why it matters: A frozen display, failed boot, bad battery, or software fault can interrupt invasive pressure or advanced hemodynamic monitoring.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact failure mode before replacing parts or sending the unit out. Ask staff what happened: Touchscreen not responding. Screen blank. Unit will not power on. Unit starts but freezes. Battery not charging. Battery drains quickly. Software error or lockup. Reboot loop. Check whether the issue occurred on battery power, AC power, during startup, or during use. Note any displayed error messages or system prompts. Expected outcome: The issue is identified as a touchscreen, power, battery, boot, or software behavior. Why it matters: “Won’t work” can mean anything from a bad outlet to a locked touchscreen to an internal system fault.

steps.title: Check External Power First
steps.instructions: Verify the monitor has reliable AC power. Confirm the power cord is fully seated at the wall outlet and monitor power supply connection. Inspect the power cord for cuts, bent prongs, loose strain relief, or damage. Test the outlet with another device or outlet tester if available. Plug the HemoSphere monitor into a known-good hospital-grade outlet. Confirm any power supply indicator light is present, if applicable. Expected outcome: The monitor powers on normally or shows signs of charging when connected to known-good AC power. If the issue resolves, document the outlet or power cord issue and stop. Why it matters: Boot and battery complaints are often caused by poor AC connection, damaged cords, or failed charging from the outlet side.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Cord and External Power Supply
steps.instructions: Check for obvious accessory-related power problems. Inspect the AC power cord and external power supply for physical damage. Look for signs of overheating, smell, cracked housing, fluid exposure, or loose connectors. Swap with a compatible known-good power cord or power supply only if approved by your facility and compatible with the device. Recheck startup and charging behavior. Expected outcome: The unit powers and charges normally with a known-good power source. If the issue follows the cord or power supply, remove the defective accessory from service and stop. Why it matters: A defective external power supply can look like a battery failure or system boot failure.

steps.title: Check Battery Seating and Charging Behavior
steps.instructions: Evaluate whether the battery is installed, seated, and charging properly. Confirm the battery is present if the reported issue involves battery operation. Power the unit from AC and observe the battery/charging indicator. Leave the unit connected to AC long enough to see if charge status changes. If your department has a known-good compatible battery, test with it according to facility practice. Check for battery swelling, damaged contacts, heat, odor, or leakage. Expected outcome: The monitor recognizes the battery and shows normal charging behavior. If the battery is damaged, swollen, overheating, or not recognized, remove it from service and follow facility battery handling procedures. Why it matters: Battery faults can cause unexpected shutdowns, short runtime, charging alarms, or failure to operate during transport.

steps.title: Perform a Safe Power Cycle
steps.instructions: Attempt a controlled restart only when it is safe to interrupt monitoring. Confirm the monitor is not in active patient use. Power the unit down normally if the touchscreen or controls allow it. Disconnect AC power. Allow the unit to fully shut down. Reconnect AC power and restart the monitor. Observe whether the system boots normally or returns to the same fault. Expected outcome: Temporary software freeze clears and the system starts normally. If the problem resolves, run a basic operational check before returning to service. Why it matters: A locked interface or frozen system may clear with a controlled restart, but recurring lockups should not be ignored.

steps.title: Check for Touchscreen Obstruction or Contamination
steps.instructions: Inspect the touchscreen for simple physical causes. Look for fluid, gel, adhesive residue, cracked glass, or pressure against the screen. Remove any screen cover, label, or object pressing on the display. Clean the touchscreen using facility-approved cleaning methods. Dry the screen completely. Test touchscreen response across multiple areas of the display. Expected outcome: Touch input responds normally after cleaning and removing obstructions. If the issue resolves, document contamination or obstruction and stop. Why it matters: Touchscreens can become unresponsive or erratic from residue, fluid, pressure, or damaged screen surfaces.

steps.title: Check for Physical Damage or Fluid Intrusion
steps.instructions: Look for evidence that the monitor should not be returned to service. Inspect the display, housing, ports, battery area, and connectors. Look for cracks, impact marks, missing covers, bent pins, residue, dried fluids, or corrosion. Check for unusual heat, smell, buzzing, clicking, or fan noise. Do not continue testing if there are signs of electrical damage or fluid intrusion. Expected outcome: The monitor is either physically safe to continue checking or removed from service. Why it matters: Fluid or impact damage can cause intermittent boot issues, touchscreen failure, power faults, and safety hazards.

steps.title: Disconnect Nonessential Accessories and Modules
steps.instructions: Determine whether an attached module, cable, or accessory is affecting startup. Remove patient cables and nonessential accessories. Disconnect external communication cables if not needed for the power-on test. Leave only the required power connection. Restart the monitor. Reconnect accessories one at a time if the unit boots normally. Expected outcome: The monitor boots normally with accessories removed, or the same fault remains. If the issue appears only when a specific accessory is connected, remove that accessory from service and test with a known-good replacement. Why it matters: A failed cable, module, or connected accessory may cause startup errors, communication faults, or system instability.

steps.title: Check Startup Sequence and Error Behavior
steps.instructions: Observe exactly where the boot process fails. Power the unit on from known-good AC power. Watch for: No power or no display. Logo appears then freezes. Reboot loop. Error message during startup. Touchscreen inactive after boot. System loads but locks up. Record the exact message, screen, and timing. Expected outcome: The failure point is clearly documented for repair evaluation. Why it matters: “No boot” and “freezes after loading” point to different causes and require different repair paths.

steps.title: Check Date, Time, Network, and Configuration Symptoms
steps.instructions: For software or communication-related complaints, verify the issue is not configuration-related. Confirm the system date and time are reasonable. Check whether the issue started after a move, network change, software update, or configuration change. Confirm network or EMR/export issues are not being mistaken for a system software failure. If the monitor works normally offline but fails when connected to the network, escalate through Clinical Engineering and IT workflow. Expected outcome: Configuration or network-related symptoms are separated from true monitor boot/software failure. Why it matters: Some “software issue” reports are actually network, destination, configuration, or interface problems.

steps.title: Run a Basic Functional Check After Recovery
steps.instructions: If the unit starts normally, verify basic operation before returning it to service. Confirm the display is stable and readable. Confirm the touchscreen responds consistently. Confirm AC power and battery status display correctly. Confirm installed modules or cables are detected. Check for active system alarms or error messages. Verify the monitor can remain powered on without freezing during observation. Expected outcome: The monitor operates normally without recurring fault behavior. If the issue does not return and the unit passes basic checks, return it to service per department policy. Why it matters: A reboot alone is not enough. The monitor must demonstrate stable operation before patient use.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported the Edwards HemoSphere monitor would freeze during startup and the touchscreen would not respond after boot.
CCR.Cause: Verified known-good AC power and power cord; battery was recognized, but the system repeatedly locked up during startup with accessories removed.
CCR.Resolution: Removed HemoSphere monitor from service, labeled Out of Service, documented startup behavior, and sent for bench evaluation/repair.

helpfulDetails: Whether the issue occurred on AC power, battery power, or both
Power cord or power supply swapped
Screen blank, frozen, dim, or damaged
Exact startup screen or error message
Whether issue occurred after cleaning, transport, software update, or network change
Unusual heat, smell, sound, or fluid exposure
